title: How to Choose Your A-Level Subjects: A Guide to University Course Requirements
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Choosing your A-Level subjects is one of the most consequential academic decisions you will make. The three or four subjects you select will determine which university courses you can apply for, which institutions will consider you, and to some extent, which careers will be open to you later. Many students choose subjects based on what they enjoyed at GCSE or what their friends are taking. That is a mistake. Your A-Level choices should be driven primarily by the entry requirements of the university courses you might want to pursue. Here is how to choose strategically.

How A-Level Choices Affect University Admissions

British universities make conditional offers based on your predicted A-Level grades and your achieved GCSE results. The offer typically specifies the grades you need in three A-Level subjects. For competitive courses, it may also specify which subjects those grades must be in. A university might offer you a place to study Medicine conditional on achieving A*AA, with the A* in Chemistry and the A in Biology. If you did not take Chemistry at A-Level, you cannot meet the offer, regardless of your grades in other subjects.

This means your A-Level subjects must align with your intended university course. There is some flexibility. Many courses list recommended subjects rather than required subjects. But for competitive courses at selective universities, the recommended subjects are effectively required. You will not be a competitive applicant for Medicine without Chemistry and Biology. You will struggle to gain admission to Engineering without Mathematics and Physics. The earlier you identify your target courses, the more precisely you can choose your A-Level subjects to match.

Subject Combinations for Popular University Courses

Medicine: Chemistry is essential for almost every medical school in the UK. Biology is required or strongly recommended by most. The third subject can be Mathematics, Physics, or sometimes a contrasting subject like Psychology. Check the specific requirements of each medical school you are considering. Some require three sciences. Others accept two sciences and one non-science.

Engineering: Mathematics is non-negotiable. Physics is required or strongly preferred by most engineering departments. Further Mathematics is highly recommended for competitive courses, especially at Russell Group universities. The combination of Mathematics, Further Mathematics, and Physics covers almost every engineering entry requirement. Some students take Chemistry or Design Technology as a fourth subject.

Law: Law does not require specific A-Level subjects at most universities, but certain subjects strengthen your application. Essay-based subjects such as History, English Literature, Politics, or Economics demonstrate the analytical writing skills that legal study demands. Some universities prefer at least one or two of these. Avoid taking only subjects perceived as soft, such as all practical or vocational courses, if you are targeting competitive law programs.

Economics: Mathematics is strongly recommended, and for competitive courses like Economics at the London School of Economics or Cambridge, it is effectively required. Further Mathematics is an advantage but not essential. Economics at A-Level is useful but not always required, provided you have strong Mathematics grades. The combination of Mathematics, Further Mathematics, and Economics is common for top applicants. A fourth subject like History or Politics adds breadth.

Computer Science: Mathematics is essential. Further Mathematics is recommended for competitive courses. Physics or Computer Science at A-Level can strengthen your application. Some universities require A-Level Mathematics and recommend a science or computing subject as the second and third choices. Check specific requirements carefully. The computing industry values skills over credentials, but university admission is credential-driven.

English Literature: English Literature at A-Level is required by most universities. A second essay-based subject such as History, a language, or Philosophy strengthens your application. Some students combine English Literature with a contrasting subject like Mathematics or a science to demonstrate breadth, but this is not necessary for admission.

What to Do If You Are Undecided

If you do not yet know what you want to study at university, choose A-Level subjects that keep the most doors open. Mathematics is the single most versatile A-Level subject. It is required or recommended for courses in engineering, economics, computer science, physics, chemistry, and many social sciences. Taking Mathematics keeps more options available than any other single choice.

A science subject, typically Chemistry or Physics, is the second most versatile choice. Combined with Mathematics, it qualifies you for most STEM courses. An essay-based subject, such as History or English Literature, rounds out a strong general combination. Mathematics, Chemistry, and History would keep you eligible for a wide range of courses across sciences, social sciences, and humanities.

The facilitating subjects identified by the Russell Group, English Literature, History, Modern Languages, Mathematics, Further Mathematics, Physics, Biology, Chemistry, and Geography, are the ones most commonly required by selective universities. Choosing from this list maximizes your future flexibility. A combination of three facilitating subjects keeps virtually every university course open to you.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

Do not choose subjects solely because you enjoyed them at GCSE. A-Level study is significantly more demanding. A subject that was enjoyable at GCSE may feel different when the depth and volume increase. Talk to students currently taking the subject at A-Level. Ask about the workload, the difficulty, and the quality of teaching. Your enjoyment matters, but it must be informed by a realistic understanding of what the course entails.

Do not choose subjects to be with friends or because you like a particular teacher. Friends change classes. Teachers leave. Your subject choices stay on your UCAS application permanently. They should reflect your academic interests and your university goals, not your social preferences.

Do not choose four A-Levels unless you have a specific reason. Most universities make offers based on three subjects. A fourth subject adds workload without necessarily strengthening your application, unless it is Further Mathematics, which is valued for quantitative courses, or unless you are genuinely torn between two subject areas and want to keep both options open. For most students, three well-chosen subjects studied in depth is better than four studied superficially.

The Bottom Line

Choose your A-Level subjects based on the entry requirements of the university courses you might want to pursue. For Medicine, Chemistry and Biology. For Engineering, Mathematics and Physics. For Law, essay-based subjects. For Economics, Mathematics. If you are undecided, choose facilitating subjects, with Mathematics as the single most versatile choice. Avoid choosing based on GCSE enjoyment alone, social preferences, or teacher loyalty. Three well-chosen A-Levels are better than four that spread you too thin. Your subject combination is the foundation of your university application. Build it carefully.
